context: Context: Campus and Workplace Small Groups (Philippines)
icebreaker_text: Share about a time when you felt lost in the dark, literally or figuratively. Maybe you got lost in a mall, or you didn't know what decision to make. How did you find your way?
big_idea: Jesus doesn't just show us the light. He is the Light that transforms our darkness into hope.
key_verse: As long as I am in the world, I am the light of the world.
section1_title: Jesus Meets You in Your Darkness
section1_content:
We all know what it feels like to be confused, lost, or stuck. In John 9, Jesus met a man who had been blind since birth. This man didn't just need physical healing. His whole life had been shaped by suffering and rejection. When the disciples asked Jesus who was to blame for the man's blindness, Jesus gave a shocking answer: "Neither this man nor his parents sinned, but that the works of God should be revealed in him" (John 9:3). Jesus reframed suffering not as punishment but as an opportunity for God's glory to shine through.
Think about Siddhartha Gautama, who later became known as Buddha. He lived 2,500 years ago and saw suffering everywhere: sickness, aging, death. He left his palace searching for answers, trying to find enlightenment through meditation and self-discipline. He wanted light. But here's the difference: while Buddha searched for understanding about suffering, Jesus came to personally encounter it and heal it.
Jesus doesn't ask you to figure everything out on your own. He doesn't say, "Meditate until you understand." Instead, He says, "I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life" (John 8:12). When you're confused, hurting, or stuck, Jesus doesn't give you a philosophy. He gives you Himself. He meets you right where you are and brings hope that you can't manufacture on your own.
section1_question: When have you felt like you were searching for answers in the dark? What did that feel like? How does it change things to know that Jesus comes to us in our darkness instead of waiting for us to find Him?
section1_key_truth: He meets you right where you are and brings hope that you can't manufacture on your own.
section1_interactive_question: When have you felt this way?
section2_content:
The man born blind in John 9 didn't just receive healing. He received a community. Before Jesus healed him, he was isolated, rejected, seen as cursed. After Jesus healed him, he boldly defended Jesus in front of religious leaders, even when it cost him (John 9:34). He found not just sight but also purpose and belonging.
Buddhism teaches that enlightenment comes through personal effort: the Noble Eightfold Path of right understanding, right speech, right action, and so on. It's all about what you do on your own. But Christianity is different. We don't walk alone. Romans 12:5 says, "So we, though many, are one body in Christ, and individually members one of another." We need each other.
This is why I-Grow groups matter. When someone in your group is going through something dark (breakup, family problems, failure sa school), you can reflect Jesus' light to them. You can say, "Hey, you're not alone. Let's pray. Let's figure this out together." That's what it means to be a community of light. We carry each other when the weight gets too heavy. And together, we become brighter.
section2_question: Who in your life has been a light to you during a difficult time? How can our group be a safe space for people who are struggling?
section2_interactive_question: How can we support each other?
section3_title: Shining Light in a Dark World
section3_content:
Jesus said in John 9:4, "We must work the works of him who sent me while it is day; night is coming, when no one can work." Notice He said "we," not just "I." Jesus invites us to join Him in His mission of bringing light into dark places. The man who was healed didn't keep quiet about what Jesus did for him. Even when the religious leaders kicked him out, he kept telling his story (John 9:25: "One thing I do know, that though I was blind, now I see").
Today, there are people around you (classmates, officemates, friends) who are searching for meaning just like Siddhartha did. They might be into mindfulness, meditation apps, or self-help books, trying to find peace on their own. But deep down, they're looking for something, or Someone, real.
Matthew 5:14-16 says, "You are the light of the world... Let your light shine before others, so that they may see your good works and give glory to your Father who is in heaven." You don't need to have all the answers. You just need to share what Jesus has done for you. Maybe it's inviting someone to I-Grow. Maybe it's praying for a friend who's stressed. Maybe it's just showing kindness when no one else does. When you shine Jesus' light, people notice. And when they ask why you're different, you get to point them to Him, the true Light that never goes out.
section3_question: Who in your circle might be searching for peace or answers right now? What's one small way you can reflect Jesus' light to them this week?
section3_key_truth: And when they ask why you're different, you get to point them to Him, the true Light that never goes out.
section3_interactive_question: Who needs this in your circle?
key_insight: God isn't just a concept or a force. He's a relationship. Father, Son, and Spirit exist in perfect love, and They invite you into that love. You don't have to earn it. You don't have to figure it all out. You just have to say yes. And when you do, you become part of God's mission to show the world what real love looks like: not distant, not detached, but close, personal, and life-changing.
action_step:
This week, reach out to one person who seems stressed or lost and offer to pray for them or simply listen. Share with your group next time how it went.




One God, Three Persons
One God, Three Persons
Icebreaker If you could describe God in one word, what would it be? (Don't overthink it, just say the first word that comes to mind!)
Big Idea God is one, yet relational: three Persons united in love, inviting us into that relationship.
Passage & Key Text
; John 8:56-58 (ESV) "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one." – Deuteronomy 6:4


Knowing the One True God
One of the biggest questions in every religion is: Who is God? In Islam, which we'll explore this week, the answer is clear: Allah is one, and nothing can be like Him. Muhammad, the founder of Islam, emphasized strict monotheism (belief in one God) and rejected anything that seemed like polytheism (belief in many gods). This makes sense in his context. He saw people worshiping idols and wanted to call them back to the one true God.
But here's where Christianity offers something deeper. Deuteronomy 6:4 says, "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one." In Hebrew, this is called the Shema, and Jewish people recite it daily. But notice the words used: "Elohim" (a plural word for God) and "Echad" (meaning one, but a unified one, like how a husband and wife become "one flesh"). This hints at God's relational nature. He is one God, but He exists in community: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
How does it feel to know that God is relational, not just powerful?
Why does this matter for you personally? Because it means God isn't distant or detached. He's relational. He doesn't just demand obedience; He invites you into relationship. When Jesus said, "Before Abraham was, I AM" (John 8:58), He was claiming to be the same God who spoke to Moses in the burning bush (Exodus 3:14). Jesus is fully God, yet He became fully human to reach you. That's not just theology. That's love in action. And that's the God who wants to know you, not just be known by you.
What difference does it make that Jesus became human to reach us?


Unity in Diversity
The Trinity is a mystery we'll never fully understand, but it teaches us something beautiful about community. God exists in perfect unity (Father, Son, and Spirit) yet each Person is distinct. They don't compete; they complement each other. John 17:21 records Jesus praying for His followers: "That they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be in us." Jesus wants us to reflect the same kind of unity that exists within God.
In the Philippines, we have a saying: "Bayanihan," the spirit of communal unity and cooperation. But even bayanihan can fall apart when there's conflict or selfishness.
What makes it hard for groups to stay united? (Think about past experiences.)
The Trinity shows us a better way. It's not just about working together; it's about loving each other so deeply that we become one in purpose and mission. 1 Corinthians 12:12 says, "For just as the body is one and has many members, and all the members of the body, though many, are one body, so it is with Christ."
In your I-Grow group, you'll have different personalities, different struggles, different gifts. But you're all united by Jesus. When someone shares a burden, you carry it together. When someone has a victory, you celebrate together. That's what it means to reflect the Trinity in community: unity in diversity, love in action.
How can understanding the Trinity help us love each other better?


Sharing the Relational God
Here's where the rubber meets the road. Many people, especially Muslims, struggle with the idea of the Trinity because it sounds like Christians believe in three gods. But that's not what we believe. We believe in one God who exists in perfect relationship. And that makes all the difference when we share our faith.
When you talk to a Muslim friend, they might ask, "How can God have a Son? Doesn't that make God... human?" But here's the thing: the term "Son" isn't about biology. It's about relationship. God didn't give birth to Jesus; God became Jesus to bridge the gap between Creator and creation. Philippians 2:6-7 says, "Though he was in the form of God, [Jesus] did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men."
How would you explain the Trinity to a friend who's never heard of it?
Your mission isn't to win arguments. It's to introduce people to the God who loves them so much that He entered their world. When you live out that love (when you forgive like Jesus forgives, when you serve like Jesus serves), people will ask, "Why are you different?" And you get to say, "It's because I know a God who didn't stay distant. He came close."
What's one way you can show God's relational love to someone this week?


Key Insight
God isn't just a concept or a force. He's a relationship. Father, Son, and Spirit exist in perfect love, and They invite you into that love. You don't have to earn it. You don't have to figure it all out. You just have to say yes. And when you do, you become part of God's mission to show the world what real love looks like: not distant, not detached, but close, personal, and life-changing.
Action Step
This week, have a conversation with someone from a different faith background (or no faith at all). Listen to their view of God, and gently share why Jesus being both fully God and fully human matters to you.
struggles_list:
Felt confused or lost
Going through a dark season
Searching for meaning
Feeling isolated or alone
Doubting God's presence
Struggling with purpose
safespace_list:
Listen without interrupting
Pray for each other regularly
Check in during the week
Share our own struggles first
No gossip rule
Celebrate each other's victories
